name: Strava
overview:
Strava is a fitness tracking platform where athletes record GPS-tracked activities (runs, rides, swims), share
them with followers, compete on segment leaderboards, and analyze their performance over time. The system
must process GPS tracks efficiently, compute performance metrics (pace, elevation, power), match activities to
predefined route segments, and serve a social feed of followers' activities. GPS data is large and requires
efficient storage and processing.

inScope: Compute activity metrics (distance, pace, elevation, heart rate)
Match activity to route segments and compute segment times
Social feed of followed athletes' activities

items: Support 100M athletes with 10M activities uploaded per day
Activity processing pipeline: metrics and segment matching complete within 60 seconds of upload
99.9% availability; brief processing delays are tolerable over data loss
GPS processing must handle noisy coordinates and variable track lengths accurately
Segment leaderboards updated within seconds of a new effort being recorded

entities.name: Activity
entities.schema:
{
  activity_id: UUID,
  athlete_id: string,
  type: enum(RUN|RIDE|SWIM|HIKE),
  name: string,
  start_time: timestamp,

  duration_seconds: int,
  distance_meters: float,
  elevation_gain_meters: float,
  avg_pace: float,
  avg_heart_rate: int,
  gps_track: string,  // encoded polyline
  status: enum(PROCESSING|PUBLISHED)
}

entities.name: Segment
entities.schema:
{
  segment_id: UUID,
  name: string,
  sport_type: enum(RUN|RIDE),
  start_latlng: [float, float],
  end_latlng: [float, float],
  distance_meters: float,
  elevation_gain: float,
  kom_time: int  // seconds
}

endpoints.response: { segment_id, entries: [{ rank, athlete_id, time, date }] }

approaches.title: Synchronous Processing
approaches.description: Process the GPS file synchronously during upload. Return metrics in the upload response. Processing a 1-hour run (3600 GPS points) takes 2-5 seconds. Users wait for the response. If processing fails, the upload fails.
approaches.tradeoff: Long upload latency; tight coupling between upload and processing.

approaches.title: Async Processing with Polling
approaches.description: Accept upload immediately, process asynchronously. Client polls for status. Better UX but polling adds unnecessary requests. Client must implement polling logic.

approaches.title: Async Processing with WebSocket/Push Notification
approaches.description: Accept upload immediately (202 Accepted). Process asynchronously via Kafka pipeline. Push notification when processing is complete. The pipeline: parse GPS file → compute metrics → match segments → generate map → publish activity. Processing is parallelized: metrics computation and segment matching run concurrently.
approaches.tradeoff: Push notification requires maintaining connection or device token; processing pipeline adds

approaches.title: latency.
approaches.description: Segment Matching Compare Every Activity Against Every Segment For each new activity, compare its GPS track against all segments. With 1M segments and 10M activities/day, this is 10 trillion comparisons/day — infeasible.
approaches.tradeoff: O(activities × segments) is infeasible at scale.

approaches.title: Geohash-Based Segment Index
approaches.description: Index segment start/end points by geohash. For each activity, find segments whose start point is within the activity's bounding box. Reduces candidates from 1M to ~100 per activity. But still requires comparing GPS tracks against 100 segments.
approaches.tradeoff: Still requires track comparison for each candidate segment.

approaches.title: Geohash Index + Dynamic Time Warping
approaches.description: Use geohash index to find candidate segments. Use Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) to match the activity track against each candidate segment. DTW handles GPS noise and slight route variations. Only compute DTW for segments whose start point is within 50 meters of the activity track. Segment matching is parallelized across multiple workers.
approaches.tradeoff: DTW is O(n×m) per segment; parallelization required for acceptable latency.

components.name: Upload Service
components.responsibility: Accepts GPS files; stores in S3; publishes processing job to Kafka

components.name: Activity Processor
components.responsibility: Parses GPX/FIT; computes metrics; generates map polyline; publishes to

components.name: Segment Matcher
components.responsibility: Finds candidate segments via geohash index; runs DTW matching; records segment efforts

components.name: Feed Service
components.responsibility: Serves social feed; fan-out on write for regular athletes; fan-out on read for pros

components.name: Leaderboard Service
components.responsibility: Maintains segment leaderboards; updates on new segment efforts

components.name: S3
components.responsibility: Stores raw GPS files and processed activity data

components.name: PostgreSQL
components.responsibility: Stores activities, segments, segment efforts, athlete profiles

components.name: Redis
components.responsibility: Segment geohash index; leaderboard cache; feed cache

questions.title: 2. Segment Leaderboards
questions.content:
Segment leaderboards rank all athletes by their best time on a segment. The leaderboard is stored as a Redis
sorted set (score = time in seconds, member = athlete_id). When a new segment effort is recorded, the
leaderboard is updated if the new time is better than the athlete's previous best. The top 10 entries are cached
in Redis for fast leaderboard queries. Full leaderboards (1000+ entries) are served from PostgreSQL.

questions.title: 3. Social Feed
questions.content:
Strava's social feed uses a hybrid fan-out approach similar to Instagram. Regular athletes (< 10k followers) use
fan-out on write. Professional athletes (> 10k followers) use fan-out on read. The feed cache stores activity_ids
per athlete, sorted by start_time. At read time, activity details are fetched from the Activity DB and hydrated with
athlete profile data.

questions.title: 4. Heatmaps
questions.content:
Strava's global heatmap shows aggregated GPS tracks from all athletes. This is computed by a batch job that
processes all activities, extracts GPS points, and aggregates them into a tile-based heatmap (Mapbox tiles).
The heatmap is updated weekly. Privacy zones (areas around athletes' homes) are excluded from the heatmap
to protect privacy.

questions.title: 5. Personal Records
questions.content:
Strava tracks personal records (PRs) for each athlete: fastest 1km, 5km, 10km, half marathon, marathon. PRs
are computed by the Activity Processor after each activity. The processor uses a sliding window over the GPS
track to find the fastest sub-track of each target distance. PRs are stored in PostgreSQL and displayed on the
athlete's profile.

Strava — System Design

S3
GPS files

Redis

Feed Svc
PostgreSQL
activities+segs
upload GPX/FIT
GET /feed
store file
publish job
consume
match segments
write activity
update leaderboard
read feed cache
keyFlows: ① Athlete uploads GPS file → Upload Svc → S3 + Kafka job
② Activity Processor: parse → compute metrics → encode polyline
③ Segment Matcher: geohash index → DTW matching → record efforts
④ Leaderboard updated in Redis sorted set (score = time)
⑤ Feed Svc: fan-out on write (regular) / read (pro athletes)

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study Dynamic Time Warping (DTW): how it measures similarity between two time series of
different lengths, the O(n×m) dynamic programming algorithm, and how FastDTW achieves O(n)
approximation.
Expert Note: Learn about GPS data formats: GPX (XML-based) and FIT (binary, used by Garmin) formats,
how they encode GPS tracks with timestamps and sensor data.
Expert Note: Understand Google Encoded Polyline format: how it encodes a sequence of lat/lng
coordinates as a compact ASCII string using delta encoding and base64.
Expert Note: Study Mapbox tile architecture: how vector tiles work, how to aggregate GPS points into
tile-based heatmaps, and how to serve tiles efficiently at different zoom levels.
Expert Note: Learn about the Haversine formula: how it computes great-circle distance between two GPS
coordinates, and when to use the Vincenty formula for higher accuracy.
